messages.content:
Customer Profile Customer: JP Morgan Chase (JPMC) Department: CTO Group - Infrastructure & Platform Engineering Industry: Financial Services Use Case Category: Infrastructure Optimization & Cost Management Interest: Hardware Migration (Intel→AMD), Performance Benchmarking, Data Platform Consolidation Pain Points Infrastructure & Cost Challenges

High infrastructure costs on legacy Intel hardware: Running thousands of compute-intensive applications on aging Intel Xeon processors with high licensing costs, power consumption, and limited performance per dollar. Need to evaluate AMD EPYC processors for cost savings without sacrificing performance. No standardized performance benchmarking framework: Each team runs ad-hoc performance tests with different tools, metrics, and methodologies, making it impossible to compare results across applications or hardware platforms objectively. Lack of visibility into workload characteristics by hardware platform: Cannot easily identify which applications would benefit most from migration, or quantify performance differences between Intel and AMD architectures for specific workload types (database, compute, memory-intensive).

Data Platform Fragmentation

Data scattered across disparate systems: Performance metrics exist in Kafka topics, cost data in financial systems, application logs in Splunk, infrastructure metrics in Prometheus, and historical data in Hive data warehouse—no unified view. No real-time correlation between cost and performance: Cannot answer critical questions like "What is our cost per transaction on Intel vs AMD?" or "Which applications have the worst performance-to-cost ratio?" without manual data aggregation across multiple systems. Delayed insights from batch processing: Hive queries run on day-old data, making it impossible to identify performance degradation or cost anomalies in real-time during migration activities. High operational overhead managing multiple observability tools: Teams spend significant time context-switching between Prometheus, Grafana, Splunk, Kafka consumers, and Hive queries instead of analyzing insights.

Key Metrics & Data Sources Hardware Performance Metrics (via Metricbeat/System Module)

CPU Utilization by Architecture:

system.cpu.total.pct grouped by host.cpu.model.name and host.architecture Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 vs AMD EPYC 7763 comparison Core utilization patterns, SMT efficiency

Memory Performance:

system.memory.used.pct, system.memory.actual.used.bytes Memory bandwidth (DDR4 vs DDR5) Page faults and swap activity by architecture

Disk I/O Performance:

system.diskio.read.bytes, system.diskio.write.bytes IOPS by disk type (NVMe SSD performance characteristics) Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) by host architecture

Network Throughput:

system.network.in.bytes, system.network.out.bytes Network interface utilization by hardware platform Packet loss and retransmission rates

Application Performance Metrics (via APM Agents)

Transaction Throughput:

transaction.duration.histogram - latency distribution by host Transactions per second (count aggregated per minute) Breakdown by service.name and deployment.environment (intel-prod vs amd-staging)

Transaction Types by Business Function:

Payment processing transactions Trade execution latency Risk calculation jobs Customer authentication services Batch processing throughput

Error Rates & SLA Compliance:

event.outcome: failure percentage by architecture SLA violation count (transactions >500ms, >1s, >5s thresholds) 4xx/5xx HTTP response codes by backend service

Distributed Trace Performance:

End-to-end span duration across microservices Service dependency latency by infrastructure tier Database query performance by host platform

Benchmark Metrics (Custom Instrumentation)

Standard Benchmark Suites:

SPEC CPU 2017: Integer and floating-point performance scores TPC-H: Database query performance (22 analytical queries) Sysbench: MySQL/PostgreSQL OLTP throughput Apache Kafka Performance: Producer/consumer throughput, latency Redis Benchmark: Operations per second, GET/SET latency Linpack: Floating-point operations (GFLOPS)

Custom Financial Workload Benchmarks:

Trade settlement processing rate (trades/second) Risk calculation engine throughput (VaR calculations/minute) Payment validation latency (p99 processing time) Fraud detection model inference time (ms per transaction)

Stress Test Results:

Maximum sustained throughput before degradation Time to process 1 million transactions Concurrent user capacity (simulated load testing) Recovery time after system restart

Infrastructure Cost Metrics (via Custom Beats/API Integration)

Hardware Cost Attribution:

cloud.instance.type or host.type: Intel vs AMD SKUs Hourly compute cost by instance: cost.hourly_rate Power consumption: power.watts (estimated TDP differences) Licensing costs: Windows Server, RHEL subscriptions by socket count

Cost Efficiency Calculations:

Cost per transaction: cost.hourly_rate / transactions.count Cost per CPU hour utilized: cost.hourly_rate * system.cpu.total.pct Performance per dollar: benchmark.score / cost.monthly TCO projections: 3-year cost models with depreciation

Resource Allocation Tracking:

labels.cost_center, labels.business_unit labels.application_id, labels.owner_team Chargebacks by department based on actual usage

Data Platform Metrics (via Metricbeat Kafka/Hive Modules)

Kafka Metrics (from existing Kafka infrastructure):

kafka.consumergroup.lag: Consumer lag by topic/partition kafka.broker.messages.in: Message throughput (messages/sec) kafka.partition.size: Topic size growth over time Topic: platform-metrics, app-transactions, cost-events

Hive/Data Warehouse Metrics:

Query execution time by warehouse (Hive, Presto, Spark SQL) Data scanned per query (cost implications) Table sizes and partition counts Historical metrics stored: hdfs.path: /warehouse/metrics/perf_*

Data Ingestion Rates:

Events per second flowing into Elasticsearch Kafka → Logstash → Elasticsearch pipeline throughput Data freshness: time delta between event generation and Elasticsearch indexing

Use Cases

Baseline Performance Comparison: Intel vs AMD Objective: Establish performance baselines across identical application workloads running on Intel Xeon and AMD EPYC infrastructure. Implementation:
Deploy identical application stacks on Intel and AMD hardware with labels: host.architecture: x86_64, host.cpu.vendor: Intel vs AMD Collect system metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network) via Metricbeat Instrument applications with APM agents to track transaction duration, throughput, error rates Run standardized benchmark suites (TPC-H, Sysbench) and ingest results as custom metrics

Stress Test & Capacity Planning Objective: Determine maximum sustainable load and identify breaking points under stress conditions for both architectures. Implementation:
Use load testing tools (JMeter, Gatling) to generate synthetic transaction load Gradually increase concurrent users/transactions per second Monitor system resource exhaustion points (CPU saturation, memory pressure, disk I/O bottlenecks) Track application behavior under stress: response time degradation, error rate spikes, queue backlog

Key Metrics:

Maximum TPS before SLA violation (>1s response time) Concurrent user capacity at 95% CPU utilization Time to recover from overload state Queue depth in Kafka topics under peak load

Cost-Performance Analysis & TCO Modeling Objective: Quantify cost savings and calculate ROI for Intel-to-AMD migration. Implementation:
Enrich infrastructure metrics with cost data: hourly compute rates, licensing costs, power consumption Calculate cost-per-transaction metrics by joining APM transaction data with cost allocation tags Build TCO models factoring in hardware depreciation, data center costs, and operational efficiency gains

Key Metrics:

Cost per million transactions processed Performance per dollar (benchmark score / monthly cost) Break-even point for migration investment Annual cost savings projection at current transaction volumes

Application-Specific Migration Prioritization Objective: Identify which applications benefit most from AMD migration to prioritize migration roadmap. Implementation:
Profile all applications by workload type: CPU-bound, memory-bound, I/O-bound, network-intensive Run pilot migrations for representative applications from each category Score applications by: (performance_improvement × cost_savings × business_criticality) Create prioritized migration wave plan

Consolidate Kafka & Hive Metrics into Unified Observability Objective: Bring fragmented data from Kafka topics and Hive warehouse into Elasticsearch for real-time correlation. Implementation:
Deploy Logstash pipelines to consume from Kafka topics: platform-metrics, app-transactions, cost-events Query historical data from Hive, then backfill into Elasticsearch indices Set up Metricbeat Kafka module to monitor Kafka cluster health (consumer lag, broker performance) Create unified data views joining:

System metrics (from Metricbeat) Cost data (from Kafka → Logstash) Historical trends (from Hive backfill)

Continuous Performance Regression Detection Objective: Automatically detect performance regressions after infrastructure changes, code deployments, or configuration updates. Implementation:
Establish baseline performance profiles for each service on AMD hardware Identify deviations: unusual CPU patterns, latency spikes, throughput drops Correlate performance anomalies with deployment events: labels.deployment.version, event.action: deploy Alert on regressions: "Payment service p99 latency increased 45% after AMD migration—rollback recommended"

Excellent! I've captured comprehensive details for your JPMC infrastructure optimization demo:

✅ **Company:** JP Morgan Chase - CTO Infrastructure & Platform Engineering
✅ **Industry:** Financial Services
✅ **Pain Points:** 7 identified (cost, benchmarking gaps, data fragmentation, real-time visibility)
✅ **Use Cases:** 6 identified (Intel vs AMD comparison, TCO modeling, migration prioritization, unified observability)
✅ **Scale:** Thousands of compute-intensive applications
✅ **Metrics:** 14 categories identified (CPU, memory, I/O, cost, benchmarks, Kafka, Hive)

Your demo profile is **100% complete**! Ready to generate your Elastic Agent Builder demo configuration.

**Type 'generate' to create your demo.**

pain_points: High operational overhead managing multiple observability tools requiring constant context-switching
No standardized performance benchmarking framework - ad-hoc testing with inconsistent tools and metrics across teams
Delayed insights from batch processing - day-old Hive data prevents real-time anomaly detection during migrations
No real-time correlation between cost and performance metrics
High infrastructure costs on legacy Intel hardware with expensive licensing, power consumption, and limited performance per dollar
Data scattered across disparate systems (Kafka, Splunk, Prometheus, Hive) with no unified view
Lack of visibility into workload characteristics by hardware platform - cannot identify migration candidates or quantify performance differences
use_cases: Application-Specific Migration Prioritization - identify which applications benefit most from AMD migration
Baseline Performance Comparison: Intel vs AMD - establish performance baselines across identical workloads on different architectures
Continuous Performance Regression Detection - automatically detect performance regressions after infrastructure changes
Stress Test & Capacity Planning - determine maximum sustainable load and breaking points under stress conditions
Consolidate Kafka & Hive Metrics into Unified Observability - bring fragmented data into Elasticsearch for real-time correlation
Cost-Performance Analysis & TCO Modeling - quantify cost savings and calculate ROI for Intel-to-AMD migration
context.scale: Thousands of compute-intensive applications running on Intel Xeon and AMD EPYC processors
metrics: Error Rates & SLA Compliance (event.outcome failure percentage, SLA violations)
Data Ingestion Rates (events per second into Elasticsearch)
Cost Efficiency Calculations (cost per transaction, performance per dollar, TCO projections)
Kafka Metrics (consumer lag, message throughput, topic size)
Infrastructure Cost Attribution (hourly compute cost, power consumption, licensing costs)
Benchmark Scores (SPEC CPU 2017, TPC-H, Sysbench, Kafka performance)
CPU Utilization by Architecture (system.cpu.total.pct by host.cpu.model.name)
Custom Financial Workload Benchmarks (trade settlement rate, risk calculation throughput, payment validation latency)
Hive Query Performance (execution time, data scanned)
Disk I/O Performance (system.diskio.read/write.bytes, IOPS, latency percentiles)
